content.method: Quasi-experimental identification using discontinuities in admission cutoffs (regression discontinuity at entrance-exam thresholds), instrumental-variable analysis using distance-to-selective-school as instrument, and cross-municipality comparison of segregation indices in areas with versus without 6/8-year track availability, controlling for residential segregation and school-choice intensity.
content.question: What is the independent causal contribution of the 6/8-year track structure itself (as distinct from free school choice, residential segregation, and church/private admissions autonomy) to Hungary's between-school segregation and SES achievement gaps?
content.why_it_matters: Nearly every reform proposal assumes tracking is a primary causal driver of segregation, but multiple lenses flag that this may instead be a sorting channel for pre-existing demand and residential/choice-driven segregation. If the track structure is not the dominant causal mechanism, structural reforms (T2, T5) will underperform expectations while carrying high transition costs.
